unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 2! In this unit you will explore celebrations and festivals from around the world, with a special focus on traditions that matter to communities across East Africa and beyond. Festivals bring people together. They remind us of our history, our values, and the things that make each culture unique. Whether it is the joy of a national independence day, the colourful lanterns of a harvest celebration, or the excitement of a local sports day, every festival has a story to tell.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Key Vocabulary section below, then study the full Vocabulary companion file.
Read the festival passage in the Reading section carefully.
Work through the Understanding questions to check your comprehension.
Study the Grammar: Prepositions for Time section and the companion Grammar file.
Complete all the Practice exercises.
Follow the Writing Workshop to write your own festival description.
Do the Listening & Speaking activities with your AI tutor or a partner.
Finish with Check Your Learning to measure your progress.

visual.alt: Learners comparing celebrations, festivals and community traditions

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Kind and generous; wanting to help others.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The benevolent woman donated food to the homeless shelter.
practiceSentences: The benevolent woman donated food to the homeless shelter.
The benevolent baker donated fifty loaves so that every family could join the harvest breakfast.
During the festival appeal, which benevolent gesture impressed you most, the donations or the volunteers' time?
"A benevolent heart gives quietly," said the teacher, "seeking no applause from the crowd."
I believe festivals reveal how benevolent a community can be when nobody is keeping score.

readings.title: Celebrations and Festivals: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 2! In this unit you will explore celebrations and festivals from around the world, with a special focus on traditions that matter to communities across East Africa and beyond. Festivals bring people together. They remind us of our history, our values, and the things that make each culture unique. Whether it is the joy of a new year festival after a long dry season, the colourful lanterns of a harvest celebration, or the excitement of a local sports day, every festival has a story to tell.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand texts about different festivals and celebrations.
Use prepositions of time (in, on, at) correctly in speech and writing.
Recognise and form compound adjectives (hand-made, brightly-coloured).
Identify participle adjectives (-ed / -ing) and use them accurately.
Write a detailed description of a festival you know or have attended.
Discuss and compare celebrations from different cultures.
Use new vocabulary about festivals, food, traditions, and community values.
Analyse poetry about celebrations and perform a short reading aloud.
Start with the Key Vocabulary section below, then study the full Vocabulary companion file.
Read the festival passage in the Reading section carefully.
Work through the Understanding questions to check your comprehension.
Study the Grammar: Prepositions for Time section and the companion Grammar file.
Follow the Writing Workshop to write your own festival description.
Do the Listening & Speaking activities with your AI tutor or a partner.
Finish with Check Your Learning to measure your progress.
Below is a preview of the most important words for this unit. You will find the full list, with definitions, word types, and example sentences, in your Vocabulary companion file. Try to learn five or six new words each day.
Reading: Festivals That Bring Us Together
Read the following passage carefully. Pay attention to the prepositions of time (in, on, at) and the vocabulary words in bold.
Every community around the world has special times when people come together to celebrate. These gatherings are called festivals, and they are woven into the rhythm of human life. Some festivals mark the changing seasons, others honour long-held customs, and still others celebrate harvests, victories, or the memory of important people. What they all share is a sense of joy, belonging, and gratitude.
In many parts of East Africa, the most eagerly awaited festival of the year is the harvest festival, which comes at the end of the long rains. Families rise early on the morning of the festival and put on their finest clothes. Children wear brightly-coloured outfits that have been hand-stitched or newly bought for the occasion. At sunrise, people walk together to the town square, greeting neighbours with warm smiles and the words “Happy Harvest!” After the opening songs, the real feasting begins. Tables are spread with freshly-made sambusas, sweet halwa, fragrant rice, and tender roasted meat. The air is filled with the rich smell of cardamom tea and the sound of laughter. Elders sit in the shade and tell stories of past celebrations, while children run through the streets, their pockets full of sweets and small gifts. On the first day, families visit relatives, and on the second day, friends gather for games and community events. It is a time of generosity: those who have more share with those who have less, and old quarrels are often reconciled.

readings.title: Celebrations and Festivals: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Far to the north, in the highlands of Ethiopia, the Festival of Rivers is celebrated in January. The festival marks the end of the cold season and the return of the melting streams, and it is one of the most colourful events in the Ethiopian calendar. Musicians wear magnificent, gold-embroidered robes, and they carry the Great Drum — a treasured copy of an ancient instrument — through the streets in a joyful procession. Crowds line the roads, singing folk songs and clapping. At dawn on the main day, river water is carried up and sprinkled over the cheering families. The atmosphere is both solemn and enthusiastic, a mixture of deep respect and pure celebration.
Meanwhile, in the coastal towns of Kenya and Tanzania, the Lamu Cultural Festival is held every year in the month of November to celebrate the long history of the Swahili coast. On the night before the main celebration, the narrow streets of Lamu come alive with lanterns, drums, and the sweet chanting of Swahili poetry. Men perform traditional stick dances, and women prepare elaborate, sweet-scented dishes. At noon the next day, scholars give talks about the town’s past, emphasising values such as compassion, honesty, and benevolence. Children listen attentively, and many feel inspired to be more diligent and kind in their daily lives.
What makes festivals truly powerful is not the food or the decorations alone, but the way they strengthen the bonds between people. In every culture, a festival is a reminder that we belong to something larger than ourselves. The next time you attend a celebration, look around: you will see authentic traditions passed down through generations, resilience in communities that keep their customs alive, and the simple, tranquil happiness that comes from being together.
Answer the following questions in full sentences. Use evidence from the passage to support your answers.
Describe what happens on the morning of the harvest festival, according to the passage.
The passage says festivals “strengthen the bonds between people.” Explain what this means in your own words.
Find and copy three examples of prepositions of time (in, on, at) from the passage. For each one, explain why that particular preposition is used.
These questions do not have a single correct answer. Think carefully and share your ideas with a partner or your AI tutor.
Find each of the following words in the passage. Write the sentence it appears in, then explain what the word means in that context.
Prepositions are small words that show the relationship between a noun and other parts of a sentence. When we talk about time, the three most important prepositions are in, on, and at. Using the correct one makes your English clear and natural.
Use in for longer periods of time:
Years: in 2026, in the 1990s
Seasons: in summer, in the rainy season
Parts of the day: in the morning, in the afternoon, in the evening
Use on for specific days and dates:
Days of the week: on Monday, on Friday
Dates: on 15 June, on the first day
Special days: on festival morning, on my birthday, on Independence Day
Use at for precise times and certain fixed expressions:
Clock times: at 9:00 AM, at half past three
Specific moments: at sunrise, at midnight, at noon
Festivals (general period): at New Year, at harvest time (when talking about the whole period)

readings.title: The Hero of Kitale, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
It was early on a Monday morning in April when Samira rose before the sun. The room was still dark, and the only sound was the soft breathing of her younger brother, Sami, asleep on a straw mat beside her bed. Their mud-brick house sat near the edge of Kitale, close to the maize farms that spread out like a green sea across the land.
Samira, now fourteen, had become known in their school as both diligent and punctual. Every teacher said the same thing: “Samira is a paragon of responsibility.” But Samira didn’t care much about praise. What she cared about was the community.
Their town had faced many problems: broken water taps, rising food prices, and a school library whose roof had fallen in during the last rainy season. Some students complained, but Samira chose to act.
Her teacher, Mr. Sami, was a charismatic man who believed in youth leadership. One day, he announced that the school would be holding its first ever Student Leadership Election. Posters appeared around the compound with names of candidates and bright slogans.
Some candidates were loud and zealous, others charming, and one boy named Adam was incredibly charismatic. Adam came from a prominent family. His uncle owned the largest hardware store in Kitale. His campaign posters were printed, not handwritten. He offered sweets to younger students and even brought a speaker to play music at lunchtime.
“Wow,” whispered Sami as the music played. “Adam is legendary.”
Samira smiled. “He may be loud, but we need a leader who is authentic.”
Samira did not have money for speakers or printed posters. What she had was a notebook, a group of close friends, and ideas. She spent her evenings creating meticulous, hand-drawn posters. They showed a tree growing from dry ground with the words: “Real leaders endure. They don’t just promise. They act.”
Her speeches were not flashy. But they were honest. “We must fix the roof,” she told the school. “We need more books. I cannot promise you music, but I promise to work.”
Samira’s words began to spread. Students whispered about how determined she seemed. Even some teachers mentioned her name in staff meetings. “That girl,” said Ms. Sophia, “has the spirit of a lion. She’s so intrepid.”
On the day of the election, the school hall was filled with a clamor of voices. Adam walked in first, wearing a brightly-coloured, fur-lined jacket. Samira walked in later, wearing her usual neat school uniform, her braids tied back, and a quiet smile on her face.
When the votes were counted, it was close. But Samira won.
Some students cheered. Others were surprised. Adam looked disappointed but shook her hand.
From that day on, Samira became a different kind of leader. She stayed late at school on Thursdays to help younger students with reading. She worked with the head teacher to organise a book drive. Her friend Adam, who was industrious, helped carry donated books every Friday.
Samira also helped reconcile students who had quarrelled. Once, two girls fought over a missing pen. Samira sat with them under the tree near the tap and helped them talk it out. “We must be impartial,” she reminded them. “We judge by facts, not emotions.”
That same week, she helped build a small garden behind the kitchen, teaching others how to grow sukuma wiki. She asked Mr. Sami to support them, and he agreed. Her leadership was quiet but steady.

readings.title: The Hero of Kitale, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
One day, Samira returned home late, tired but happy. Her mother looked up from the stove. “You look exhausted.”
Samira sat beside her. “We planted spinach today. Sami helped too. He’s becoming quite agile, you know.”
Her mother laughed. “Well, that’s good. But don’t forget to rest. Even lions must sleep.”
Despite her busy days, Samira always made time for study. She was tenacious. Even when the lights failed, she lit a kerosene lamp and reviewed her notes. Her aunt once said, “Samira will be legendary one day.”
Then one Friday in June, something unexpected happened.
A teacher’s motorcycle was stolen from outside the school gate. Suspicion fell on a new student named Adam. He was quiet, often late, and didn’t have many friends. Some students shouted, “He did it!” Others said nothing.
But Samira stood in front of the class. “Let’s not rush. He is innocent until proven guilty. We must be vigilant but also fair.”
The next day, the motorcycle was found abandoned near the river. Adam had nothing to do with it. Samira had saved him from false blame.
People began to see her not just as a student leader, but as a true role model. Even the elders in town heard of her efforts. One afternoon, a woman named Mama Lillian came to school to donate books.
“You must be the girl,” she said. “My grandson told me you’re the reason he wants to lead.”
Samira blushed. “I’m just doing my part.”
Over time, Samira’s name reached the local newspaper. An article was published titled: “The Benevolent Girl Who Changed Her School.”
One morning, the governor of the county came to visit. He walked through the classrooms and smiled when he saw the small garden. “Who started this?” he asked.
The governor turned to her. “You’re quite young. What inspires you?”
Samira replied, “Kindness, honesty, and hard work. I believe if we help one another, we all rise. It’s just benevolence.”
That weekend, Samira received an invitation to speak at the county youth forum. Her school bus arrived early on Saturday. She wore a clean green dress, hand-stitched by her aunt. It was modest, simple, and very much Samira.
At the forum, she spoke with confidence. Her words were enthusiastic, her manner calm and tranquil. She spoke about resilience, the need to push forward even when things are tough.
“Many people wait for a hero,” she said. “But what if you are the one? What if you can change just one thing?”
The crowd clapped, not because of music or fancy clothes, but because they believed her. Her speech was later posted online, where thousands watched and commented.
On the last day of the term, Mr. Sami gave Samira a letter. “Scholarship application,” he said. “For a leadership camp in Nairobi. I believe you’d represent Kitale well.”
Samira stared at the form. She had never left their county.
That evening, she sat with her family under the stars. “Should I go?” she asked.
“You should,” her mother said. “Your light is not meant to stay hidden.”
Sami, with eyes full of pride, added, “You’ll do great. And don’t forget to write!”
Samira laughed. “I won’t. But you must take care of the garden. And be punctual!”

grammar.title: How to Use Them: Step by Step
grammar.explanation:
Step 1 — Use IN for longer periods of time:
Think of in as a big container that holds a stretch of time.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Step 1 — Use IN for longer periods of time:
Think of in as a big container that holds a stretch of time.
Months: I was born in July. / The rainy season falls in different months each year.
Years: Kenya gained independence in 1963.
Seasons: The harvest festival is held in autumn.
Parts of the day: We train in the early morning. / I study best in the evening.
Centuries and decades: Great changes happened in the 21st century.
Step 2 — Use ON for specific days and dates:
Think of on as landing on a particular spot on the calendar.
Days of the week: The market is busiest on Saturday.
Dates: My birthday is on 15 March.
Special days: We celebrate Independence Day on 12 December. / We feast on the first day of the harvest festival.
Day + part of day: The guests arrived on Friday evening.
Step 3 — Use AT for precise times and fixed expressions:
Think of at as pointing to an exact moment.
Clock times: The ceremony begins at 10:00 AM.
Specific moments: The gates close at sunset. / The fireworks start at midnight.
Meal times: Let’s talk about it at lunch.
Fixed expressions: at the weekend (British English), at night, at the moment.
Festival periods (general): We visit relatives at harvest. (Here “at harvest” means during the whole harvest period.)
Special Notes and Exceptions
English has a few tricky cases. Here are the most important ones:
Night vs. other parts of the day: We say in the morning / afternoon / evening, but at night. This is an exception you must memorise.
On vs. At for festivals: “On harvest morning” (a specific day) vs. “At harvest” (the general period). Both are correct, but they mean slightly different things.
No preposition needed: We do not use in, on, or at before today, tomorrow, yesterday, this, last, next, or every. Example: “I will see you next Monday” (NOT “on next Monday”).
Twelve Real Sentence Examples
The harvest festival is celebrated in the month of October.
We always visit our grandparents on the first day of the new year.
The morning bell is rung at dawn.
She was born in 2012.
The school concert takes place on Wednesday.
The fireworks display starts at 9:00 PM.
In the rainy season, many festivals are postponed.
My cousin’s wedding is on 5 August.
We eat dinner at sunset during the summer.
The sports day is always held in November.
The parade begins on New Year’s Day.
I like to read quietly at night before I sleep.

grammar.title: Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
grammar.explanation:
Study these common errors carefully:
Mistake 1: “I was born on July.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Study these common errors carefully:
Mistake 1: “I was born on July.”
Fix: July is a month (a long period), so use in: “I was born in July.”
Mistake 2: “We have a meeting in Monday.”
Fix: Monday is a specific day, so use on: “We have a meeting on Monday.”
Mistake 3: “The show starts in 7 PM.”
Fix: 7 PM is a clock time, so use at: “The show starts at 7 PM.”
Mistake 4: “I’ll see you on the evening.”
Fix: “The evening” is a part of the day, so use in: “I’ll see you in the evening.”
Mistake 5: “She arrives at next Friday.”
Fix: Drop the preposition before “next”: “She arrives next Friday.”
Memory Trick

Practice 1: Choose the Correct Preposition
Write in, on, or at in each gap.
The festival begins ______ the 20th of March.

I finished my homework ______ the evening.

The family gathers for dinner ______ sunset.
Practice 2: Correct the Mistakes

We celebrate harvest on the end of the season.
She was born at 2010.
The bus leaves in exactly 3:00 PM.
The market opens at every morning.
I saw my friend in last Tuesday.
The programme starts on midnight.
They always travel in New Year’s Day.
Practice 3: Write Your Own Sentences

A sentence about something you do in a particular month.
A sentence about something that happens on a specific day of the week.
A sentence about something that starts at an exact time.
A sentence about a festival that takes place in a particular season.
A sentence about an event on a specific date.
A sentence about something you do at night.
Practice 4: Festival Paragraph
Write a short paragraph (five to six sentences) about a festival or celebration. You must use in at least twice, on at least once, and at at least once. Underline each preposition of time.
Example: In the month of October, my family works from dawn to sunset. On the last day, we feel excited because the harvest festival is coming. At sunrise on the morning of the festival, we put on our best clothes and walk to the market square. We eat a big meal together in the afternoon.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Festival Poster Pitch
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Imagine you are organising a community festival. Design a simple poster (on paper or digitally) and then present it to your AI tutor or a classmate. Your pitch should include:
The name and date of the festival.
Where and when it will take place (use prepositions of time!).
What activities, food, and entertainment will be available.
Why people should come.
Additional Reading: Cultural Comparisons
Festivals look different around the world, but they serve similar purposes. Read the short descriptions below and think about what they have in common.
Diwali — The Festival of Lights (India)
Diwali is celebrated in October or November. It lasts for five days and marks the victory of light over darkness. Families clean their homes, light clay lamps called diyas, and share sweets with neighbours. On the main night, fireworks fill the sky and the streets sparkle with candles. Children wear brand-new, brightly-coloured clothes and receive gifts from their elders.
Thanksgiving — A Harvest Celebration (United States)
Thanksgiving is celebrated on the fourth Thursday in November. Families gather around a large table to share a meal of roast turkey, potatoes, and pumpkin pie. Before eating, people take turns saying what they are grateful for. The day is about giving thanks for the harvest and for the people in your life.
Nowruz — The Persian New Year (Iran and Central Asia)

writing.title: Writing 1: Describe a Festival
writing.promptAndInstructions:
In this section you will plan, draft, and polish a description of a festival or celebration you have experienced or know about. Your finished piece should be at least 200 words long.
Step 1: Choose Your Festival
Pick a celebration you know well. It could be:
A seasonal festival (a harvest festival, a lantern festival, a spring festival, a new year celebration)
A national holiday (Independence Day, cultural heritage day)
A family celebration (a wedding, a naming ceremony, a graduation)
A community event (a harvest festival, a sports day, a school open day)
Step 2: Plan Your Writing
Use this planning frame to organise your ideas before you start writing.
Name of the festival: What is it called? When does it take place?
Background: Why is this festival celebrated? What is its history or meaning?
Preparations: What do people do to get ready? (clothes, food, decorations)

quizzes.question: What does 'benevolent' mean?
quizzes.options: Having a strong desire to achieve something and not giving up. | Kind and generous; wanting to help others. | Showing great excitement and interest about something. | Working hard and being careful to do things well.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Kind and generous; wanting to help others.
quizzes.explanation: The benevolent woman donated food to the homeless shelter.

quizzes.question: What does 'charismatic' mean?
quizzes.options: Hard-working and very dedicated to tasks. | Having a special charm that attracts and inspires people. | Showing great excitement and interest about something. | Working hard and being careful to do things well.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Having a special charm that attracts and inspires people.
quizzes.explanation: The charismatic leader inspired everyone to work together for a better school.

quizzes.question: What does 'determined' mean?
quizzes.options: Brave and not afraid of danger or difficulty. | Hard-working and very dedicated to tasks. | Having a strong desire to achieve something and not giving up. | Showing great excitement and interest about something.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Having a strong desire to achieve something and not giving up.
quizzes.explanation: She was determined to finish her homework before going out to play.

quizzes.question: What does 'diligent' mean?
quizzes.options: Brave and not afraid of danger or difficulty. | Hard-working and very dedicated to tasks. | Very famous and admired, often because of a special story or history. | Working hard and being careful to do things well.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Working hard and being careful to do things well.
quizzes.explanation: She is a diligent student who always completes her homework on time.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Kind and generous; wanting to help others. The benevolent woman donated food to the homeless shelter.
Having a special charm that attracts and inspires people. The charismatic leader inspired everyone to work together for a better school.
Having a strong desire to achieve something and not giving up. She was determined to finish her homework before going out to play.
Working hard and being careful to do things well. She is a diligent student who always completes her homework on time.
Showing great excitement and interest about something. She was enthusiastic about joining the school soccer team.
Hard-working and very dedicated to tasks. She is an industrious student who always completes her homework on time.
Brave and not afraid of danger or difficulty. The intrepid explorer climbed the highest mountain without fear.
Very famous and admired, often because of a special story or history. The legendary hero saved the village from the dragon.
Very careful and paying great attention to details. She was meticulous when painting her model, making sure every tiny part was perfect.
A person or thing that is a perfect example of a particular quality. She is a paragon of kindness because she always helps others.
